data.name: Creamy Avocado Hummus
data.description: A vibrant, velvety fusion of classic Middle Eastern hummus and Mexican guacamole. This bright green dip combines creamy chickpeas, rich avocado, nutty tahini, and zesty lime for a refreshing appetizer.

refrigerator.notes: Store in an airtight container with a piece of plastic wrap pressed directly against the surface of the hummus to prevent oxidation.

freezer.notes: Freezing is not recommended as the avocado will become watery and discolored upon thawing.

items.name: avocado
items.notes: use a large, very ripe avocado; yields about 150g flesh

items.name: canned chickpeas
items.notes: save the liquid or aquafaba for other uses if desired

items.name: ice water
items.notes: use as needed to adjust thickness

instructions.text: In the bowl of a food processor, combine the lime juice, tahini, garlic, and kosher salt. Process until the mixture becomes pale and whipped.
tips: Whipping the tahini and citrus juice together first creates a much smoother, creamier base emulsion.

instructions.text: Add the drained chickpeas to the food processor and process for one full minute. Stop to scrape down the sides of the bowl with a spatula, then process for another minute.

doneness_cues.visual: thick, mostly smooth paste with no whole chickpeas remaining

instructions.text: Add the avocado halves, ground cumin, and fresh cilantro to the mixture. Process until the avocado is completely broken down and the hummus turns a uniform bright green color.

doneness_cues.visual: uniform bright green color with no chunks of avocado

instructions.text: With the food processor running, slowly stream in the extra virgin olive oil followed by the ice water. Continue to blend until the hummus reaches a light, airy, and whipped consistency.
tips: If the hummus is still too thick, add an additional tablespoon of ice water and process again.

instructions.text: Transfer the avocado hummus to a serving bowl. Use the back of a spoon to create a shallow well in the center, drizzle with a little extra olive oil, and serve immediately.

troubleshooting.symptom: Hummus has a grainy or chunky texture
troubleshooting.likely_cause: The avocado was under-ripe, or the chickpeas were not processed long enough
troubleshooting.prevention: Ensure the avocado yields to gentle pressure before using, and process the chickpeas for at least two full minutes before adding other ingredients
troubleshooting.fix: Continue processing for an additional 2 to 3 minutes, adding one more tablespoon of ice water to help smooth it out

troubleshooting.symptom: The surface of the hummus has turned brown in the refrigerator
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Exposure to oxygen has caused the avocado enzymes to oxidize
troubleshooting.prevention: Always press a piece of plastic wrap directly onto the surface of the dip to block out air before storing
troubleshooting.fix: Use a spoon to gently scrape off and discard the top brown layer; the bright green hummus underneath is perfectly safe and delicious to eat

chef_notes: Ice water is the culinary secret to ultra-light, airy hummus. The cold temperature helps emulsify the fat in the tahini and olive oil while preventing the high friction of the blender blades from cooking the mixture.
Lime juice provides a brighter, more complementary acidity to the rich avocado than the traditional lemon juice used in standard hummus, bridging the flavor profile toward guacamole.

For the absolute silkiest texture, you can boil canned chickpeas for 15 minutes with a pinch of baking soda, then rinse them under cold water to remove the skins before blending. It adds active time but dramatically improves mouthfeel.
data.cultural_context: This recipe represents a vibrant modern culinary fusion, marrying the ancient Middle Eastern tradition of pureed chickpeas and sesame tahini with the rich, indigenous Mesoamerican avocado. The resulting dish acts as a delicious bridge between classic hummus and guacamole, celebrating the creamy textures and dynamic flavor profiles of both food cultures in a single bowl.
